Prediction confidence is computed by normalizing the output probabilities of tokens associated with the two options.
To account for the LLMs’ ordering bias, the LLMs are prompted twice for each example by swapping the options and computing an average confidence score.
LLM is presented with a single summary generated by itself, another LLM, or a human.
LLM is prompted to answer a yes/no question of whether a given summary is its own generation for self-recognition.
LLM is prompted to rate a given summary with a Likert-scale rating of one to five for self-preference.
The final rating is computed as the average of the five possible Likert-scale scores weighted by the output probability of each number token.
To make scores comparable across individual and pairwise measurements, the individually measured confidence and Likert-scale scores are further normalized.

We conduct human evaluation of pairwise judgment on generated summaries to check if the self-preference shown by LLMs is justified by quality differences, particularly between LLM-generated summaries
Human annotators provide judgments for each pair, resulting in 900 pairwise comparisons.
Human annotators rank the three LLMs as GPT-4 > GPT-3.5 > Llama 2.
GPT-4 has a self-preference that is disproportionate to the actual quality differences.
All three evaluator models display ordering bias where preferences are reversed when the ordering of options is reversed.
Bias is accounted for by averaging logit-based confidence scores across two orderings.
Results are discarded as 'ambiguous' when the preference displayed ordering-based reversal.

There is a strong linear correlation between a model’s self-recognition ability and its self-preference. Fine-tuning for self-recognition ability on one dataset transfers to the other.

We measure the correlation between self-recognition ability and overall dataset self-preference. For GPT-3.5 on the XSUM dataset, the evaluator prior to fine-tuning has a correlation of 0.41 (Kendall’s τ) between correctly recognizing its summary from a pair and preferring its summary from that same pair.

Every fine-tuning configuration results in a model with a positive correlation between self-recognition ability and self-preference. This correlation remains stable as self-recognition ability increases.
We ensure that the causal direction is not the reverse, as LLM evaluators do not systematically favor summaries generated by fine-tuned models over those generated by the original model.

Both concepts are studied in relation to BERT, T5, and GPT-3.5 models. There is a larger capability gap between these models affecting the quality of summarization, leading to the need for wider evaluation.
Self-preference bias is included in the study of LLM cognitive biases in a pairwise question-answering setting by Koo et al. GPT-4 is found to have lower self-preference than GPT-3.5 in this study.
GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 are compared in terms of self-preference in multiple studies. Contradictory findings exist between studies, highlighting the need for further evaluation.

GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 are evaluated for self-recognition ability on pairs of ten-sentence fables. The findings show discrepancies in the accuracy of the models, emphasizing the importance of wide experimentation on diverse datasets.
Detection of LLM-generated text is crucial for AI safety and misinformation prevention. Both areas address the importance of understanding and detecting language model outputs.
Self-recognition focuses on the introspective ability of language models.
The self-recognition task can be seen as a highly restricted version of detection where the method is limited to prompting an LLM.

Self-recognition is a general ability that can potentially affect many multi-LLM interactions, leading to safety concerns.
We discuss safety risks caused by self-recognition and its causal effect on various biases.
In model-based benchmarks, a model's rating can be inflated simply because it is similar to the evaluator model, leading to biased self-evaluation.

We focus on text summarization as a realistic problem with existing high quality data that have seen successful application of self-evaluation.
Our cross-dataset evaluation provides initial evidence that self-recognition is a general capability that can be amplified easily by fine-tuning on a small number of examples from one dataset.

Our preliminary experiments indicate that the strength of both properties are insensitive to prompts, so all conditions use the same straightforward prompt design. To reduce variance, we will expand our experiments with more prompt designs in future work.

Along the lines of fine-tuning on the general domain, we will also mix self-recognition with standard instruction following datasets to improve coverage on the spectrum of self-recognition signal strength.
